project_overview.project_name: Integrating Innovation for Rural Competitiveness in Honduras - COMRURAL II
project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve access to markets and climate-smart practices and contribute to the economic inclusion of targeted beneficiaries in select agricultural value chains
project_overview.total_financing: US$146.9 million (IDA Credit: US$75 million; Private Commercial Finance: US$53.5 million; Counterpart Funding: US$12.7 million; Beneficiary Contributions: US$5.7 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 50,700 beneficiaries including 18,000 direct beneficiaries in business plans, 1,700 beneficiaries from improved public services, and 31,000 indirect beneficiaries from partner/allied enterprises. Targets include 25% women, 10% youth (18-30 years), 10% Indigenous peoples, 5% Afro-descendants, and 10% from GoH social protection programs

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Partial - The project operates in areas with social conflicts related to agribusiness and land tenure. The Corredor Seco (Dry Corridor) region is particularly vulnerable to climate shocks and has experienced significant migration pressures. High levels of crime and violence are noted as persistent development challenges driving emigration. The project includes specific measures to address social conflicts and land tenure issues through stakeholder engagement and safeguards instruments.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Support to enhance the competitiveness, resilience, and innovation of agribusiness initiatives
key_components.description: Supports competitive, innovative, and climate-resilient agribusiness initiatives through productive alliances. Includes pre-investment activities (Subcomponent 1.1) for subproject preparation and productive investments (Subcomponent 1.2) with three grant windows: Window 1 (subsistence agriculture targeting informal groups), Window 2 (transitioning agriculture targeting formal RPOs), and Window 3 (commercially-oriented agriculture). Finances works, goods, training, technical assistance, matching grants, and agro-logistics investments.
key_components.financing: US$56.2 million IDA credit (total component cost: US$115.4 million including US$53.5 million private finance and US$5.7 million beneficiary contributions)

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Support to the modernization of Honduras' agribusiness enabling environment
key_components.description: Modernizes and strengthens public sector services and regulatory framework. Subcomponent 2.1 improves selected public agribusiness services (SENASA and ARSA) through functional reviews, ICT systems, laboratory construction, and equipment. Subcomponent 2.2 enhances public-private coordination through National Agriculture Council (NAC) establishment, National Agro-logistics Strategy, National Agro-Climatic Information System (NACIS), and legal/regulatory reforms.
key_components.financing: US$9.7 million IDA credit (total component cost: US$22.4 million including US$12.7 million counterpart funding)

key_components.description: Finances project coordination, management, monitoring and evaluation, fiduciary implementation, audits, environmental and social safeguards oversight, and implementation of Stakeholder Engagement Plan and Grievance Redress Mechanism. Includes goods, training, operational costs, and consulting services.
Provides for immediate response to eligible emergencies through reallocation of funds from the project. Reflects strategic approach taken across Honduras portfolio for emergency response.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Inversión Estratégica de Honduras (INVEST-H), a government agency under the General Coordinator of the Presidency of the Republic of Honduras
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Implementation Unit (PIU) at central level with five regional offices. Steering Committee for Component 1 oversight comprising INVEST-H, SAG, RPOs, and private sector. Committee to Enable the Environment of Agribusinesses (CEEA) for Component 2 coordination comprising SAG, SENASA, ARSA, Presidency representative, and private sector. Inter-institutional agreements with SENASA, ARSA, and SAG. Two Implementing Consulting Firms (ICFs) contracted for subproject implementation.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations (July 2016, revised November 2017 and August 2018). Prior review for high-value contracts. Post-review missions annually with sample of at least 20% of contracts. Procurement Plan prepared in STEP system. Standard procurement documents for international competitive bidding; national market approach for specific contracts. Procurement guidance for subprojects included in POM.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: INVEST-H M&E Team (3 staff) responsible for overall M&E. Management Information System (SIG) includes FM module, business plan module, beneficiary monitoring module with geo-referencing, and Results Framework control panel. Semi-annual and annual progress reports, baseline data, mid-term review, and final evaluation. Joint semi-annual supervision missions with WB. Third-party surveys for beneficiary satisfaction and technology adoption. External audits annually with interim review of first semester.

identified_risks.risk_description: Political polarization has deepened following unresolved issues from 2009 political crisis. Emerging political fragmentation and governance weaknesses may delay Congressional approval of WB projects and affect implementation pace/direction.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: WB will focus on areas of critical importance for country development and seek to support dialogue with all stakeholders based on shared development objectives. During implementation, team will promote dialogue with key stakeholders including private sector to support commitment to project and public-private approach.

identified_risks.risk_description: Increased uncertainty related to global trade, adverse weather, and lower international prices for agricultural products (especially coffee) may cause large agricultural losses and reduce exports. Persistent deficit of state-owned electricity company (ENEE) threatens fiscal position. Prolongation of tax and electricity bill amnesty weakens tax base and widens fiscal deficit. These circumstances may limit fiscal space and affect annual project budget.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: WB will closely track macroeconomic issues with GoH and adjust component activities if necessary. WB is supporting GoH to find sustainable solution to energy sector challenges through technical assistance. GoH aims to strengthen fiscal sustainability through central government consolidation, investment spending cuts, and comprehensive plan to rescue electricity system and reduce ENEE debt.

identified_risks.risk_description: Project design is more complex than ongoing COMRURAL due to financing amount, three financing windows, and beneficiary targeting. Project promotes adoption of innovative technologies and design elements that beneficiaries may not have utilized before.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project will continue to provide feedback loop to incorporate lessons learned into new calls for proposals through GRM. Will track citizen engagement and remain in close dialogue with GoH throughout project to discuss necessary adjustments in project approach.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: COMRURAL II is larger, more complex, and incorporates more innovations compared to COMRURAL. Institutional capacity risk is rated as High due to increased complexity.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project will supplement INVEST-H capacity by hiring two ICFs to implement subprojects. GoH has defined new development strategy through 2020 Plan to promote agribusiness transformation and modernization. INVEST-H has considerable experience with externally financed projects and seeks to build on successful COMRURAL model. Task team will work closely with GoH to coordinate and provide rigorous implementation support.

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk rating reflects project design complexity. Contracting with ICFs to implement subprojects poses risks for FM as ICFs may not be familiar with WB fiduciary processes. Complex inter-institutional arrangements, INVEST-H structure and workload, support and monitoring of contracts with local governments and new rural organizations, and country context contribute to risk. Market conditions, violence, and security issues could significantly impact implementation.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Close supervision of ICFs by central and regional PIU offices. PIU will have fiduciary responsibility for transferring funds to RPOs and respective accountability for subprojects in Windows 2 and 3. INVEST-H will implement FM assessment action plan agreed during preparation. POM will include detailed descriptions of ICF roles and responsibilities. Detailed FM procedures, controls, and monitoring arrangements specified in Annex 1. Additional FM staff hired at central and regional levels. External auditors contracted. Inter-institutional agreements specify roles and responsibilities.

identified_risks.risk_description: Overall environmental risk is Substantial because project involves interventions in rural areas with potential to negatively impact terrestrial and aquatic natural habitats and forests. Pesticide use represents occupational health and safety issue. Overall social risk is Substantial due to occupational risks for agricultural workers, community health and safety risks, and potential child labor risk. Social context includes Indigenous and Afro-descendant communities with history of social conflicts surrounding agribusiness sector and water use.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Borrower prepared Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF), Indigenous Peoples and Afro-descendants Planning Framework (IPAPF), Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP), and Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF), all consulted and disclosed prior to appraisal. Site-specific ESMPs will be prepared for each subproject identifying and avoiding/minimizing adverse impacts, defining mitigation procedures, establishing subproject-specific GRMs, and determining need for additional social management instruments. RPOs will receive specialized environmental and social management support from ICFs and INVEST-H. WB supervision missions will check compliance with safeguards instruments. Pest Management Plan (PMP) included in ESMF with provisions for site-specific PMPs. Exclusion list identifies ineligible subprojects. Provisions for chance finds, dam safety, and international waterways included.

identified_risks.risk_description: History of social conflicts in agribusiness sector including recent conflicts over land tenure. This could impact implementation of potential project investment activities and participation of private sector finance.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project includes Stakeholder Engagement Plan and consultation activities during preparation and implementation of business plans and other project activities. Consultative and participatory processes formed part of preparation of social management instruments with representatives of Indigenous peoples, Afro-descendants, and women's organizations. During implementation, participatory processes with beneficiary groups including women, youth, and local communities will be integrated into operational procedures.

identified_risks.risk_description: Underlying vulnerability of Honduras to extreme events outside control of investments, policy, and planning remains high. Overall adaptive capacity of country remains low. Honduras is among countries most affected by negative climate and weather events with little capacity to cope and adapt. 2015 drought affected 1.3 million people with small-scale farmers suffering average loss of 80% in agricultural production.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project includes climate-smart practices and technologies to promote resilience and address climate vulnerabilities. Climate-smart agriculture approaches prioritized in subproject selection including soil and water conservation, pasture management, improved livestock management, farmer-led irrigation, water harvesting, energy-efficient systems, and biodigesters. Water management prioritized in Corredor Seco subproject selection. Project coordinates with other WB water resource management initiatives. Ex-ante GHG analysis shows project expected to lead to net carbon sink of 1,709,185 tCO2-eq over 20 years.

identified_risks.risk_description: Project expects to mobilize US$53.5 million in private commercial financing. Risk that this capital is not leveraged in full.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project will ensure business plans developed by qualified financial service providers. Project will guarantee portion of investments through COMRURAL Guarantee Fund (FONGAC) which has operated successfully for 10 years. Ongoing COMRURAL entered into MoU with partner financial institutions providing established framework for commercial banks and micro-finance institutions to finance business plans. MoU will continue under proposed project. Project will seek to establish similar MoUs with additional financial institutions. If anticipated financing does not materialize, project is designed to be scaled down to achieve PDO.

safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - GRM established under ongoing COMRURAL in 2008 with project complaint log and filing system to monitor status and follow up on each complaint. GRM will be strengthened during project execution based on ESMF provisions. Strengthened requirement for establishing grievance mechanisms at subproject level. Complaints can be submitted by phone, through project websites (http://www.comrural.hn/buzon-de-quejas/ and http://www.mcahonduras.hn/contactenos/), or in person at INVEST-H regional offices (Santa Rosa de Copan, La Ceiba, Siguatepeque). Signs with contact information placed in subproject locations. Borrower will provide biannual reports to WB on GRM. Results Framework includes indicator to monitor grievances. Communities and individuals can also submit complaints to WB Grievance Redress Service (GRS) or Inspection Panel.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender strategy based on literature on barriers to gender equality in rural Honduras and agribusiness sector (low female labor participation, lack of opportunities, earning differentials) and COMRURAL experience. Project addresses gender gaps on three levels: (1) Business profile preparation includes gender gap analysis addressing differentiated roles, factors of production, barriers, social/family dynamics, and female-specific needs; (2) Business plan selection allocates additional points to organizations with females on Boards and minimum 30% female-headed households; (3) ESMPs include activities to address identified gender gaps. Target of 25% female beneficiaries and 25% of jobs created going to women, monitored through Results Framework indicators.
vulnerable_groups: Beneficiaries of GoH social protection programs (target 10%)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project addresses conflict risks through multiple mechanisms: (1) Stakeholder Engagement Plan with consultations involving Indigenous peoples, Afro-descendants, women's organizations, rural producer organizations, government agencies, financial institutions, and civil society; (2) Indigenous Peoples and Afro-descendants Planning Framework (IPAPF) to guide screening and assessment of potential adverse social impacts; (3) Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) for potential land-related issues; (4) Grievance Redress Mechanism at project and subproject levels to address conflicts; (5) Emphasis on productive alliances and market-driven approaches to reduce tensions; (6) Specific targeting and inclusion measures for historically marginalized groups to promote economic inclusion and reduce grievances; (7) Environmental and Social Management Framework with provisions for managing social conflicts related to water use and agribusiness activities.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Honduras faces multiple fragility factors: (1) High poverty (64% of households at or below poverty line) and extreme inequality (Gini Index 0.50); (2) High informal economic activity (75%) with low productivity and earnings; (3) Persistent high levels of crime and violence driving emigration; (4) Political polarization and governance weaknesses following 2009 political crisis; (5) Vulnerability to climate shocks and natural disasters (Hurricane Mitch caused 81% of GDP in damages; 2015 drought affected 1.3 million people); (6) Agricultural sector vulnerability with 72% of agricultural families in semi-subsistence farming; (7) Social conflicts over land tenure and agribusiness sector; (8) Limited institutional capacity and fiscal constraints; (9) Weak public services and regulatory frameworks for agribusiness. Project designed to address these fragility factors through economic inclusion, climate resilience, institutional strengthening, and improved enabling environment.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security issues identified as potential implementation risks: (1) Market conditions, violence, and security issues could significantly impact implementation of activities; (2) High levels of crime and violence are persistent development challenges driving emigration; (3) Social conflicts in agribusiness sector including land tenure disputes could impact project investment activities and private sector participation; (4) Project operates in 13 departments with varying security contexts. Mitigation measures include: (1) Close coordination with local governments and communities; (2) Stakeholder engagement to build support and reduce conflicts; (3) Flexible implementation approach to adapt to security conditions; (4) Regional offices to provide on-ground presence and coordination; (5) Grievance mechanisms to address community concerns; (6) Emphasis on inclusive and participatory processes to build local ownership.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Potential for displacement: Yes - Involuntary Resettlement Policy (OP/BP 4.12) triggered. Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared, consulted, and disclosed prior to appraisal to guide screening and assessment of potential resettlement impacts during project execution. If resettlement foreseen for specific subproject, Borrower through INVEST-H must prior to carrying out subproject: (a) prepare, disclose and carry out consultations of pertinent resettlement action plan acceptable to WB; and (b) implement pertinent resettlement action plan or abbreviated plan in accordance with terms and manner acceptable to WB. Legal covenant requires this process. Refugee/migration context: Honduras experiencing growing exodus due to poverty, inequality, crime, and violence. Climate shocks (2018 drought) recognized as driving farmers to migrate. Project aims to address migration drivers through economic inclusion, job creation, and climate resilience in agriculture sector.
